title: Chicken saag
subtitle: Chicken in silky spiced spinach sauce
excerpt: Chicken pieces simmered in a smooth, deeply spiced spinach sauce. The spinach is blended with the onion and spice base before the chicken is added, so the sauce is fully integrated and silky rather than textured. Chicken saag occupies the same menu position as lamb saag but cooks in half the time, a weeknight-friendly version of the spinach curry format.
sourceNotes: Chicken saag (palak chicken) is a Punjabi dish applying the spinach-blending technique of palak paneer to chicken. It is documented in Madhur Jaffrey's cookbooks and has been on British Indian menus since the 1970s alongside the lamb version.

content.text: Chicken saag uses the same technique as saag paneer and lamb saag but with chicken thighs, which cook in about 20 minutes rather than the 75 to 90 minutes required for lamb shoulder. The spinach sauce is built first (onion caramelised, spices added, spinach wilted and blended) and then the chicken is simmered in it until cooked through. The spinach sauce is the vehicle; the chicken is the main component.

The spinach must be blended until properly smooth, any visible spinach strands in the finished sauce indicate insufficient blending. A stick blender in the pan is the most practical approach. The blended sauce should be dark green, smooth, and thick enough to coat a spoon before the chicken is added.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 8 minutes until golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{chilli-green}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{spinach}} in batches, wilting each addition before adding the next. Once all the spinach has wilted, blend until completely smooth with a stick blender.
Cook the chicken in the spinach sauce

Return the pan to medium heat. Add the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} pieces to the blended spinach sauce. Stir to coat, add 100 ml water, and cover. Simmer for 18 to 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through, stirring occasionally. Remove the lid for the final 5 minutes to reduce the sauce to a coating consistency. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and {{double-cream}} if using. Season with salt.

items.fix: Blend for an additional 30 to 60 seconds. The sauce should be completely smooth, any visible spinach indicates incomplete blending
items.symptom: Sauce has green strands of spinach

items.fix: Wilt the spinach only until just soft, do not overcook. Blend immediately after wilting to preserve the green colour
items.symptom: Sauce khaki coloured rather than dark green

items.fix: Thigh meat holds moisture better in this sauce. Pull at exactly 18 to 20 minutes, chicken thighs at this size cook through cleanly in that time
Add hot water 50 ml at a time and stir. The sauce should be pourable but coating, thicker than a broth, less thick than a paste

content.text: Chicken saag is the quickest of the saag family of dishes. It takes less than 45 minutes from start to serving, while lamb saag requires nearly two hours. On the British Indian menu it sits alongside lamb saag and saag paneer as the third member of the spinach-sauce trio, each distinguished by its main protein. The chicken version is the most practical for weeknight cooking; the blended spinach sauce can be made ahead and the chicken added fresh to order in 20 minutes.
